chain_of_thought: Crash in certificate parsing stack: Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum → Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT → Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT → CERT_Load → JNI → fuzz harness.
First native app-level frame is Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum within libUbikeyJni.so, which is application library, not allocator/sanitizer.
Tool calls to search_functions_by_name failed (404), so detailed decompilation is unavailable; must reason from symbol names and context only.
Function names strongly suggest custom ASN.1 and X.509 certificate parsing logic; Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum likely walks ASN.1 structure to count or index child elements.
Crashes in ASN.1 child-count computation are classic sources of memory safety issues (out-of-bounds, integer overflow, invalid pointer walks) when fed malformed or untrusted certificate data.

CERT_Load and Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT indicate that untrusted certificate blobs are parsed into internal structures; in realistic app usage, these are typically remote or file-provided (e.g., server certs, policy data).

JNI bridge Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetPolicyOID exposes this parsing chain directly to Java; fuzz harness likely replaces the normal Java caller but does not invent an impossible path—policy OID parsing from cert is a plausible app feature.

Because the exact crash reason (e.g., SIGSEGV, abort, assertion) and instruction are not shown, we cannot prove specific corruption, but Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum is far from harness-only or purely defensive abort code.

No evidence that crash is due to unrealistic harness behaviour like forced NULLs; rather, fuzz likely provided a malformed ASN.1 blob triggering a logic/memory bug in the child-number computation routine.

Given the function semantics and parsing stack, the most plausible interpretation is a bug in ASN.1 traversal logic that can be triggered by malformed certificate input; this yields at least a realistic DoS, and possibly memory corruption depending on implementation.
Without decompilation we cannot assign a precise CWE, but out-of-bounds read/write (CWE-787/125) or improper validation of data length (CWE-20/130) are the most likely classes.
Thus, this crash should be treated as likely representing a real, input-driven vulnerability rather than a pure harness/environment artefact, though details and exploitability remain uncertain.
reasons: Crash occurs deep in ASN.1/X.509 certificate parsing logic in app library, not in harness-only code
Function Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum almost certainly walks attacker-controlled ASN.1 structures and can be influenced by malformed certificate data
No indication that crash is due to artificial harness inputs like impossible JNI arguments
Even if memory corruption cannot be confirmed, malformed certs clearly can trigger a native crash via this path (realistic DoS)
recommendations: Decompile and audit Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum and related ASN.1 parsing routines for bounds checks on lengths, indices, and child counts
Add strict validation of ASN.1 lengths and structure before traversal; reject malformed certificates early
Instrument with ASan/UBSan and re-run fuzzing focused on certificate/policy OID parsing to pinpoint and fix concrete memory errors
assumptions: jGetPolicyOID ultimately parses ASN.1/X.509 certificate or similar untrusted data from network or file
The crash is caused by malformed ASN.1 structure triggering logic/memory bug in Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum, not by an artificial harness-only condition like forced NULL JNI env
The same native path is reachable in production builds under realistic input
limitations: Unable to decompile libUbikeyJni.so due to tool error; crash instruction and exact failure mode unknown
No visibility into Java-side caller of jGetPolicyOID or how input bytes are sourced/validated
Cannot distinguish between out-of-bounds access and defensive abort inside Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum

exploit.trigger_method: Malformed certificate or ASN.1 blob processed via UbikeyJni.jGetPolicyOID
prerequisites: Attacker can cause the app to process attacker-controlled certificate/ASN.1 data (e.g., via network connection or imported file)
Native UbikeyJni library with vulnerable ASN.1 parsing is present and jGetPolicyOID is reachable in the app flow
exploit_pipeline: Craft an X.509 certificate or ASN.1 structure with malformed child element layout to stress Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum (e.g., inconsistent lengths, excessive child count)
Deliver the crafted certificate/blob to the application through any supported channel that eventually calls UbikeyJni.jGetPolicyOID (network handshake, imported cert, internal policy load)
Trigger the app workflow that invokes jGetPolicyOID so that CERT_Load and Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT run on the malformed data
Observe app crash or undefined behaviour during Compute_ASN1_ST_ChildNum execution; refine payload shape with fuzzing or differential testing to explore potential memory corruption
poc_commands: adb shell am start -n com.kbankwith.smartbank/.MainActivity --es cert_path /sdcard/Download/crafted_cert.der
exploit.notes: Exact crash type, offset, and controllability are unknown without decompilation and more detailed traces; current assessment is based on function naming and stack context suggesting unsafe ASN.1 traversal.

chain_of_thought: Crash is in __memcpy called from Decode_DER_ASN1_ST within libUbikeyJni.so; this suggests an out-of-bounds or invalid pointer during ASN.1/DER parsing.

Call stack: fuzz_one_input → Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetPolicyOID → CERT_Load → Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT → Decode_DER_ASN1_ST → __memcpy. All relevant native frames are in the same library, indicating app code rather than system/harness libraries.

The operation is DER ASN.1 decoding of what is very likely attacker-controllable certificate/policy data; this kind of logic commonly copies variable-length fields based on lengths read from the ASN.1 stream. A memcpy crash there is typical of insufficient bounds checking on those length fields.

Although I couldn’t decompile the exact functions due to tool errors, naming and stack structure are highly indicative: Decode_DER_ASN1_ST presumably parses a DER-encoded ASN.1 structure, computing lengths and copying into internal buffers via memcpy, likely trusting lengths without full range checks against the available buffer.

CERT_Load and Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT appear to be higher-level wrappers that transform raw certificate bytes into a parsed structure; the crash at the low-level decoder implies that malformed DER (from fuzzer, and in real life from untrusted certificates/tokens) can trigger memory corruption.

The JNI entry Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetPolicyOID suggests that Java passes in some raw certificate or policy blob and receives an OID string. There is no indication that Java length-checks the internal ASN.1 fields; at best it might cap the overall buffer length, which still leaves room for internal length overflows leading to memcpy writing/reading beyond allocated memory.

Because the crash is a direct memcpy fault in app’s ASN.1 parsing code reachable from the JNI used in production, this is almost certainly a genuine memory safety issue (out-of-bounds write or read). Even if the current fault is a read, it’s a strong indicator of a broader class of unsafe copies controlled by ASN.1 lengths.

Reachability in real-world: any feature that loads X.509 certs/keys/tokens/policies from network, QR code, NFC, files, or a remote server could feed arbitrary DER into CERT_Load, exposing this to remote or semi-remote attackers.

Severity is high: a memcpy-based overflow in parsing complex encoded formats is classically exploitable for code execution, though proving practical RCE would need more detailed analysis (heap layout, mitigations). For triage, we can conservatively treat it as high-severity memory corruption with at least denial-of-service and potential RCE.

Confidence is less than 0.9 due to lack of direct decompiled view, but the function naming, call pattern, and specific crash site in memcpy during DER decode makes it very likely a real vulnerability rather than a harness artifact.
reasons: Crash occurs in __memcpy called from Decode_DER_ASN1_ST inside app library libUbikeyJni.so, consistent with out-of-bounds copy during ASN.1 parsing.

JNI path Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetPolicyOID → CERT_Load → Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT → Decode_DER_ASN1_ST shows attacker-influenced DER data being decoded without evidence of robust bounds checks on internal ASN.1 lengths.

Memcopy-based crashes in DER/ASN.1 certificate parsing are a well-known pattern for exploitable memory corruption (CWE-787/CWE-119), and this is part of production authentication/crypto JNI, not the fuzz harness itself.
recommendations: Decompile Decode_DER_ASN1_ST and its callers to identify all memcpy/memmove operations and ensure length arguments are validated against both source buffer size and destination capacity before copying.

Add strict ASN.1 length and structure validation before performing any copies, including checks for overflows when computing offsets/lengths; reject malformed DER early instead of proceeding to memcpy.
Enable and run with ASan/UBSan or hardened allocators during testing to systematically find other unsafe memory operations in the certificate parsing and JNI layers.
assumptions: The DER/ASN.1 data being parsed in Decode_DER_ASN1_ST originates from untrusted or semi-untrusted sources such as remote servers, user-supplied certificates, or tokens.
Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetPolicyOID does not fully re-encode or sanitize ASN.1 internal length fields and thus passes attacker-controlled structure into CERT_Load.
The crash in __memcpy is due to an out-of-bounds access (invalid dst/src/len) and not a spurious harness-side memory misuse.
limitations: Unable to decompile libUbikeyJni.so functions due to MCP tool errors, so argument provenance and exact bounds checks cannot be shown in code snippets.
No direct view of Java side (UbikeyJni.jGetPolicyOID) to confirm exact parameter types/validations or how untrusted inputs reach this JNI.
Exact nature of the memcpy fault (over-read vs over-write, heap vs stack) and surrounding mitigations (stack canaries, ASLR, hardened malloc) are unknown, so precise exploitability cannot be proven.

exploit.trigger_method: Supplying a crafted DER-encoded certificate or policy object that is loaded through the UbikeyJni jGetPolicyOID path.
prerequisites: Attacker can cause the app to load or process a DER/ASN.1 certificate/policy blob via UbikeyJni.jGetPolicyOID or related API (e.g., by controlling a server certificate, smart card content, or imported file).
App is built with vulnerable version of libUbikeyJni.so and without full memory safety mitigations (e.g., no full CFI, default allocator).
exploit_pipeline: Craft a malicious DER-encoded ASN.1 object (certificate or policy) with inconsistent length fields that cause Decode_DER_ASN1_ST to compute an incorrect copy length or pointer.

Deliver this object to the application through a realistic channel (e.g., import a certificate file, connect to a malicious server, or present a forged token) that results in a call to UbikeyJni.jGetPolicyOID.
When CERT_Load invokes Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT and Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, the malicious lengths trigger __memcpy with an out-of-bounds src/dst/len, corrupting heap or stack memory.
Iterate on payload structure (and potentially heap grooming) to convert the crash into controlled memory corruption that can hijack control flow or at minimum produce a reliable denial-of-service.
